I'm not sure if a govt agency is capable of that sort of 5D chess, but its an interesting story.

author: @tonyponsford
text:
Hi Rob, and of course this is 1 half of the story. Endothermic reactions with say ammonium nitrate cool down, which are useful in a hot country like Thailand. Basically, a chemical aircon unit, using the sun to dry the chemical and distilling water into the room to apply to chemical there. Cant seem to work out a good method of moving the crystallised chemical back into the room. Nearest so far is a rotating plate, half in the room and half in sun....but i will keep banging my head on this !!!!     
All the best,   Tony

author: @wayne1959
text: Gooday Robert. A story from Aust about my trucking days and quick lime. I used to load roadtrains of it to take to mines in the northern territory in 20kg bags off a conveyor and onto pallets. In my day the trucky had to load 22 ton of the stuff onto each trailer and they generously helped me lay wooden pallets on the deck to load them onto. Anyway, after loading all those bags fresh from the mill and covered in a fine dusting of the stuff, some of it eventually made its way into my skin pores on my shirtless shoulder and by the time i had loaded both trailers totalling 45ton i had quite the sweat up and in a day or two from the mill on my 2400km journey my shoulders would shed skin like a bad sunburn does and by the time i returned for my next load around 6 days later i would have to go through the rather painful ordeal over again. After a few complaints by myself and other drivers about the problem, they issued us with soft leather shoulder covers held on by a strap around our necks and under the armpit not used for loading. Well after less than an hour of wearing them we had terrible chaffing and extra bad lime burns in the raw skin and one poor guy needed hospital treatment for his infected wound. After all this they finally decided it was better to have the pallets machine stacked and then loaded onto our trucks with a forklift to our joy. They then changed to large one ton bags on pallets which continues today more than 30yrs later. We often chat about those days before oh&s went crazy and wonder how rich we would be in todays world should a similar thing happen. Sorry but this just reminded me of those times..

Between that and clearing the scrub for cropping, it's created an ecological disaster !

author: @petergambier
text:
Thanks for doing this program Robert. I buy in finely 'kibbled' quick-lime which I slake, pronounced slack here in Somerset, into my lime putty. For every tub of quick-lime I add 2 tubs of water and get back 2 tubs of lime putty which I mix with the sand for my mortar.

You can of course do what is called a 'hot-mix', this is where you mix the sand (or chalk aggregate) and quick-lime together with a little water, mix it up and leave covered for 24 hours or use straight away. It's preferred by masons and wallers because it expands in the joints I'm told. 

I normally slake my lime and leave it in a tub with a little water to cover it and use it days, weeks, months or years later. 

The Romans left their putty to mature 4 years before using it. The advantage of Roman mortar over other mortars is weight and no doubt a lovely sticky mix. Without it they could not have built the oldest building in the world with a domed roof, the Pantheon.

They also unwittingly discovered a mineral (not that they knew it) called Phillipsite which is produced when lime mortar blocks are submerged in sea water for 5 to 10 years. Under stress the Phillipsite bends rather than shatters and explains why Roman harbour sea defense walls are as strong today as they ever were and just like adding fibers to a mortar it gives it a stronger tensile strength.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Calcium carbonate: also used as an antacid.  I'm going to microwave some Tums :)

This could also be a way to store solar thermal energy for later use if you use a solar dehydrater to evaporate the water.  Night winds may also aid in dehydration overnight if there's not a lot of humidity in the ambient air.

Could maybe be used to directly heat a house in winter using radiant heated floorboards

Could also use the heat in a hey box to cook food.  Or bury it in the ground under a Dutch oven. 

Fireless cooking... I can imagine a sort of calcium powered barbecue pit, but like a slow cooker. Just add water instead of lighting the fire. The water wouldn't even have to be clean, but as a side function, you could condense the steam into something drinkable.

author: @VinoVeritas_
text: Hi Rob. I believe the reaction actually releases roughly 8.5 X the amount of kilojoules. So it's nearer 57kj per 50g as opposed to the 6.7kj per 50g as mentioned in the video. I've been investigating several methods of cheap seasonal thermal storage lately and have looked at Sodium Acetate Trihydrate, Calcium Chloride Hexahydrate and Sodium Sulphate Decahydrate among others as options. Having watched this video, I'm now going to compare the above with Calcium Oxide + H2O.

text: A very important tip for using lime (or concrete): wash your hands before going to the bathroom. The skin on your hands is relatively more resistant to chemicals than most of your skin, and the skin on your fun bits is much much less resistant.
